speaker: interviewer
text: This is Sonia Zyroff. I am Marsha Blitstein. This is Bloomingdale, Illinois, USA. And this is tape number five. Mrs. Zyroff, you were telling us about your son, the synagogue he belongs to, and how Holocaust survivors were discussed and were dealt with in relation to Tisha B'av and that being uncomfortable.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to hear it?

speaker: interviewer
text: I would just-- I would-- yeah. If you could just fill us in a little bit on that more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, that synagogue is in La Jolla, California. I think that other [INAUDIBLE]. We might have the address. I think the rabbi's name is [INAUDIBLE], something like that. [INAUDIBLE] rabbi. And he has all the young-- young people, mainly young married couples, young couples. And-- and every one of them, sort of-- he does not observe the Holocaust. I was there that time and I asked. And he said-- the rabbi came over to me and said, Mrs. Zyroff, I'm sorry. We don't observe that. We combine it with Tisha B'av.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I spoke to some young people, young kids there. You know anything? No. The rabbi doesn't say nothing about a Holocaust. We don't know about a Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I talked to a boy what attends the shul. And he says-- I says, well, then your-- your rabbi is not a good Jew if he doesn't do that. So you tell him I said so. He doesn't fit to be a rabbi, because he has to teach you there was a Holocaust and tell you about it. Yeah. I talked to one of the boys down there. And I got really, really burned up. And I wouldn't want-- I didn't want to tell my son this. But the rabbi said that he doesn't observe it. I held that inside me for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I contributed [NON-ENGLISH] to the shul. And I wanted-- first, I gave them in honor of my son and so forth. Then I bought six more [NON-ENGLISH]. And I wanted them to put my family who perished in the-- and I wanted them to put down they were killed in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were fighting me, my son for several weeks before they decided until I said, if they don't want to put killed in the Holocaust, then I'm taking the [NON-ENGLISH] back. I'm not going to-- going to leave them down there. So they didn't want to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm confused here until I talk to another lady who belongs to that synagogue. Because she was from South Africa. And I said-- she says, didn't you know that they don't believe in Holocaust in that synagogue? So then I really got bitter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told my son, if they don't going to put in, I'm taking. So they put something in. They told me they did, but they worded it differently. But they keep those [NON-ENGLISH]. To be honest with you, I don't want to even keep my name of my killed family in a shul like that. I-- I don't-- I don't want it. And I don't know how to handle that. He is my son that I love dearly. And he's close to me. I'm-- I'm fortunate and lucky in the world to have my kids close to me. And I don't want to hurt him. And he supports that synagogue. And he's a big man, conducts the services every year on Rosh Hashanah, the first day, and reads the Torah and goes around with the Torah. And they give me [NON-ENGLISH]. But if they don't want to know about the Holocaust, those are not my kind of Jews. So I'm really bitter about it. And I-- it bothers me day and night. I wish I know what to do about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what you're doing today-- what you're doing today is-- is certainly a big help in letting the world know. Letting the world know your story is extremely important.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I'll have a tape from what I'm talking today, I'd have my son show it in the synagogue to the rabbi. Let him hear it, what I am saying, that I'm bitter. Last year, I didn't go to-- I couldn't tell my kids that I don't want to come because of that. So I took off for Israel for the holidays. I told them I'm going to Israel. I can't come. I didn't want to come, not for that reason. I could have gone to Israel anytime later. I just left, because I didn't want to come into that shul. See, it's just not my shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: But again-- again, today, what you're doing-- goes against that kind of thought.

words.text: goes against that kind of thought.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope it'll do me some good while I'm still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just hate to see Jews, that they want to erase it, packaged it away with a Tisha B'av. Tisha B'av, destruction was a couple of times. What happened to 2,000 years of the story with a-- with a-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: A horror happened in my life, the biggest ever the Jewish history. To combine it with this? I can't live with it. It bothers me. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to do about it. I don't want to be on bad terms with my kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Well, what you're doing is the best thing to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was right.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's to continue to tell your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son-- my son didn't believe me in the beginning when I finally told them that, that rabbi [INAUDIBLE]. So what do I do? I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I go with my son to shul early in the morning, and I get a hold of that rabbi. And I says, rabbi, will you do me a favor? Tell my son the same thing you told me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. I remember that conversation. I'm going to tell him. And after davening, the rabbi went over to shake my son's hand. And he told my son, it's true. I told your mother that I combine it with Tisha B'av. My son feels very, very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. That's difficult. Mrs. Zyroff--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --if you could tell us something about the accomplishments that you've-- that you've had since you've come to this country. Tell-- tell us something about those.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think if-- I shouldn't say if. I have to tell you this. I went just in February 19 and 20, was a 50 years of Holocaust survivors gathering. We celebrated the 50 years it's after. Elie Wiesel had his final talk. And he appealed to us by saying, make peace with God. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It got to me. And I thought, why did he have to say that? I prayed to God all the time in my hiding. But then again, I felt so funny. I just thought, God, you saved-- saved my life. What about my baby, my rest of the family? Why? Just why? So some of the Holocaust survivors turned to religion. Some of us turned against it. And it's-- it's hard, but I kept-- I didn't tell my children until they grew up when they heard me talking about the Holocaust. Really? Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they were growing up, I didn't mention a word. I felt guilty to bring them in to a world like that. They would tell me, why did you have more children for-- for to-- to be somebody the world can kill? The whole world was looking at that and nobody said, boo, don't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to our rabbi in time with distress. We had a rabbi in the city. People couldn't get it. Couldn't go down in the ghetto, couldn't do nothing. But one man at a time will go and ask rabbi, what's going to be the end of it? So he come back. And he says, my [NON-ENGLISH], I cannot tell you nothing. All I can say [NON-ENGLISH]. You understand? Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our God took off for America. He's not with us anymore. So-- and somehow, before they killed everybody in the ghetto, they took the rabbi out. And they tell them to put the tallis and tefillin in front of his grave and-- and say his prayer. And they shot him before they did everybody else in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I have questions why? Because nobody can give me the answer why. I mean, why? So I kept a nice home. As you can see, my son grow up very religious. The-- the rabbis took interest in him. He's very learned. Do you have other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my daughter, yeah. I have a daughter. Her name is Judy. And she's married to-- it's a situation. My son married an American-born girl. And her name is Ellen [INAUDIBLE], very nice girl, has a PhD in English, a master's degree in library science. My daughter married a boy from Holocaust survivors. His name is also Joel Riff. Ours spells with two F's, my daughter-in-law's with two F's, and my son-in-law's with two F's. [LAUGHS] It's coincidentally.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Joel and Judy live in Palo Alto, which is a suburb of San Francisco. And after they-- of course, Judy graduated Johns Hopkins. Then she went University of Chicago. She was a business administrator flying from coast to coast, handling big companies like Procter Gamble, and Kellogg's, and RCA in the business administration field. She was doing really well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her husband went back to school. And he became an attorney in computer fields. All that happened in Chicago. And then she moved to-- she had to give up her job. When Joel graduated, he found a job in Palo Alto in his field, computer lawyer. And he's doing well. And my daughter had to quit her job, because she had to move. She got pregnant and had another baby, a little boy. And Adam is going to be bar mitzvah in December. And Daniel is six years young. And my daughter has two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the kids, thank God, are well and everybody's doing fine. That's very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Except, as I say, I feel bad that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Except your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: --husband couldn't be with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. That certainly is sad. I would, though, before we conclude, be interested for you to share some of your personal accomplishments as far as working.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I was home at the time. I-- in the beginning when I came to America, I was going with my baby on my shoulders working, working by doing day work by the day to help ourselves out. Not scrubbing floors, but I helped, like, fixing up drapes and doing something like that for $0.40 an hour for a long time. And then after a while, of course, I was busy organizationally. And we had a fashion show for [INAUDIBLE]. I was in charge. And I was-- so anyway, I worked and then finally got to-- somehow to the television station. And in 1973, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was called to come to CBS. Well, I talked about it. I was teaching already in the sewing field. And then Montgomery Ward want to advertise. So Montgomery Ward was pouncing my show.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were looking somebody who knows to sew that can advertise. So the station found me, called me. And they told me to come for a date, took me in a dark room. And first, they-- they taped me by telling a little something. And then they ran my tape. And the leaders were there, [INAUDIBLE] and the boss. And they said, how do you like what you did on television? I says, I don't know if I like it. I think I have to go and take elocution lessons before I can speak a good English. So he said to me, that's what we like about it. And that's what we want. And you are hired. So I had my television show from 1973 to 1981 every Wednesday. I have pictures of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was called Every Wednesday, they were wheeling in, like, a house with all the equipments, ready with the sewing machines. So I made sketches. I went by this way. People were writing letters and asking questions by the load. So I picked out the questions I liked to answer that make sense, one after another. And I made sketches. I brought the blackboard with me. It shouldn't take time, because time was limited. And I was explaining, answering this lady's questions and-- and that one. And then people were sending self-addressed stamped envelopes. They wanted the answers. So-- but there were the times that 300 letters went out-- went out for a week. And this is-- was my program, just answering questions on the air with the sketch and a writer. Little I knew that the station is saving all my answers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I see an ad in the paper. Montgomery Ward is selling a sewing machine, but they give us an extra one of Sonia's sewing books. Sewing books? So what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They picked out all my answers and put it in a small booklet. And they gave it away with the sewing machines. [LAUGHS] So then later, I started saving them for myself. And I'm finishing up now, I'm going to have three books sponsored. So-- and I think I brought some of the books with me. I don't know if I brought them here or not. I know I put them in. Maybe I left them at the-- I was so confused the last few days after you called that I don't know what to do first, getting to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, three books came out. And I want to put them now in one. So it's going to be one big book. So I have it all done on sewing tips. That's--

speaker: interviewee
text: It says KDLH TV Duluth, Sonia's Sewing-- Sewing Tips and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. It's wonderful that you've been so successful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- I'm still having a class when I feel like it. If I go away for the winter, I cancel. I do what I feel like it. But I do. And believe it or not-- you're going to laugh. I had a class last fall making-- making challah, Jewish-- Jewish-- Jewish braiding challah tips on the air. And I had a class full of people. Non-Jewish women came to learn how to make the braiding of the holiday seasoned bread, decided to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: How do I started that? A cousin from-- from Minneapolis was at my house. And she wanted to know how. So they came first to tape me at home. And then later, I thought, I'll show it on television, too. They send me the pictures of all of that with-- with the tape that I did. So I did that, too. So anytime I want to go on and do something on the air, they always have room for me to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: But right now, my husband got sick right in 1980. And I had a big job caring for him. The beginning was the hardest. I had him home. I traveled with him. And I had lot of problems with him. And then finally, he's 11 years already nursing home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. That part-- that part is sad. But we are glad that you shared your story with us today. This is really wonderful. I just want to say thank you on behalf of the foundation and on behalf of everyone who's-- who's going to see this all over the world. This is a wonderful thing you've done.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it-- you did for me so much. Because I feel I'm not dying. I'm going to be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's for sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going be there to the rest, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's for sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: My story will be needed for the future generations. That make me feel very good. I thank you so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mrs. Zyroff. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: You ready? Well, this picture was given back to me by a cousin in Canada who had it way before, many years. Because I had nothing left originally from home when I was running away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is my mother, Edith and my father, Jacob. This is my oldest brother, Hersch, who was married, had a wife and three children. And this is my second brother, Hyman. He was married, had wife and children. He was killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: This one was a soldier drafted in the Russian army. They never came back. This is me right in the back. And this is my dad, Jacob, and my baby sister, Esther. They were all killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken in the 1930s, because the family looks so young here. So this-- this brother was hiding for a long time. And then they found him, and he was running. And they shot him. And he was lying in the fields. Animals ate him up. Well, this is a paper that's called the [INAUDIBLE]. It was going out from Montreal, New York-- and-- Canada, I mean. And it's written, [NON-ENGLISH], which means 'those are all my memories from the Nazi years.' And that article was running two weeks in the [INAUDIBLE]. And through that paper, a relative of ours from Argentina responded. And we started corresponding with each other. And I was real happy. After the war, if you lose everybody, anybody who identifies himself as a relative is more than a sister or brother. That picture of me. But that's what they do.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's me the day after my daughter was born. I wasn't fat. I don't know how come the picture's so big. This is my baby. Was born in the States, 1951. Judy is-- is her name. And then my doctor was a very nice lady. And she knew my story as a doctor. And she put my experience in an article in the paper, which is called 'Judy is Safe, This Is the USA.' Because my older baby girl was killed by the Nazis. So-- and then she knew all about it. And she gave everything without me. And it appeared in the paper, 1951, when my daughter was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is me with an article in the Duluth paper. A day after I complained, because they had a big Nazi leader come to the university campus. And they gave him big headlines, everything against the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I called the newspaper. And they come to take my story which took several pages of my experience during the Nazis. So the newspaper did a big article for me as a response to a Nazi-- Rockwell was his name, if I call. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the university was bringing up-- they gave the Nazi the big headlines in the first page, and then came a communist. They gave him a little corner at the end of the-- of the newspaper with his response. So I was bothered by that. And I called, and I had my article and my explanation about the good-- good deeds the Nazis did for me during the war. Just tell me, this-- this girl--

speaker: interviewer
text: And begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So as you probably all know, this is Menachem Begin. It was taken at his house in '70s when I visited. This-- this girl was the hostess of Israel. She was meeting all the dignitaries coming from all over, from all the other countries to Israel. And that's me in the '70s. It was a birthday party at Menachem Begin's family. His granddaughter by the name of [INAUDIBLE], was seven years old. So we were invited to come to that party.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My father, Alfred, in the left side. And of course, I am in the middle, and my daughter, Judith. That was at the Holocaust survivors gathering in 1951. It was a worldwide gathering at the Wailing Wall in Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your husband on the left hand side?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't say my husband? I said my father? Did I say my father? Oh, god.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, what's your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Alfred, who is sick right now, couldn't be with us-- in a nursing home. And that's me and my daughter, Judith. And the picture was taken of us. That picture appeared in the [INAUDIBLE] that year. We went to Israel to a Holocaust survivors gathering worldwide in 1981.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my husband, Alfred. And it's me in the middle. So the girl next to, we don't know. So all of the Holocaust survivor at that gathering came to the Wailing Wall to light a candle. And there was some people saying prayers for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was also 1981.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Horochow, a plaque from our city and memory put down of all the Jews being destroyed there. And my husband and I went specially to take pictures with our plaque. So here I am from my hometown. Next one will be my husband and his hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this plaque located?

speaker: interviewee
text: At [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here we are. My son, Jack, who is a doctor [INAUDIBLE] in La Jolla and was very honored, he wanted to go with me. And that's me there. We are saying Kaddish. That's a Ukrainian cemetery in the bushes where they told us that my parents were killed after hiding 15 months. They told us that's where they were buried. So we thought we going to have something to take out, transfer. But there was nothing left, just one big hole. I almost fell in. So my son and I were saying Kaddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year as this taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: You may begin to tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Here is a monument of 4,000 Jews from our city. I was shocked when I found the crosses on it to disguise that there's no Jews there. And they put down that their people, 4,000 of their people laying here. Well, I couldn't take that. I was there in hiding. And I knew there were only 4,000 Jews. The grave is a couple miles long. And here is where they buried all the city-- the Jews from our city. It's a mile or two long, the whole grave. And people were telling us that the Earth was shaking for three months after, because they buried the Jews alive. But what hurt me so that they disguised it and put that their people. Well, I couldn't make peace with that. And I said, you take me better right away to the mayor of the city. I better make it that they admit what they did. Here I am. Here I am at the mayor's office. My son's doing the taping. And our discussion is that there was only Jews. After a struggle with him several times, then he finally decided there was only Jews. And he's willing to put a Jewish monument and take that one off.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he needs money. So I asked him, all the houses we saw, the Jewish houses are still there. The people don't live there. Are they paying rent for these houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I think it's very little. I say, charge them the right price. And you take the money and buy a monument to put up on the Jewish grave. So the mayor that time tells me there was also 300 young men, a separate grave, that they took out in 1941 when they just came in, all the young man. And they locked them up in a special room, giving them only salt herring to drink-- to eat. That's what the mayor told me himself. They were hoping they will die from starvation without-- from the hydration, without giving them water to drink. But eventually, they had to dig their own graves. And they shot all 300. There was a separate grave from all the 300 young men.

speaker: interviewer
text: You may begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I am in the middle. There's two sisters. This one was Anya. And this one was Manya. And Anya was 17 at the time. And Manya was there. She was the main to keep me there for about nine months. After I went through a lot of horrors, I came to her and she was hiding me for nine months. So we went back there also in '92 to visit them and appreciate what they did for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You may begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My daughter, Judy, her son Daniel, and her son Adam, who's going to be bar mitzvah in December, and my son-in-law Joel Riff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my granddaughter, [INAUDIBLE], who graduated Columbia in New York--

speaker: interviewer
text: She's on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: --with high honors on the left. Of course, this is me next. That's my son, David, who is now a student University of Chicago. My daughter-in-law Ellen, PhD in English, master's degree in library science. My son, the doctor at Scripps Clinic in La Jolla graduated Johns Hopkins and Columbia in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just like that. And tell us about this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: About this now. This is the paper, as you see. My picture there, holding the flag. That was the flag where I became citizen of the United States. I'm still keeping it as a very important for me to remember. And that article appeared on the 4th of July on the first page in the only, the biggest Duluth newspaper. It was all the writing about my experience also in the United States and in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell them what to do. We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello. This is my son, Jack, my son, the doctor. I'm proud of it. And I'm sorry, but his wife, Ellen, and the children, they didn't know about it at the last minute. They should have been here with me also. But I'm happy Jack reminded. And we are happy to take that picture together. He's the one who traveled with me, wouldn't let me go alone to Europe. He went with me and took great big film of the experience of during the war and after the war. So anybody who is interested to see and hear, just look for the tapes and-- and the pictures, whatever they will appear, so you'll know a little bit more about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very proud of my mother for doing this. And I've-- I've actually taken my own interviews of her lasting about six hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I've done some various other videotaping. I'm sorry that my sister isn't here. And I'm sorry that my father isn't here. My father is very ill, unfortunately, today. And it would also have been nice if-- if my wife and some of the other members of the family could have been here today as well. But some of this came on short notice. And, you know, that's unfortunate. But I don't know that I have a lot to add. I'm sure she's covered everything very thoroughly, because I know she always does things very thoroughly. Growing up in Minnesota was-- or particularly in Duluth, Minnesota, was very different, I think, than most people's experience in that it was a very, very much of a gentile community. And we were thrust into that community in 1947 almost right from the ashes of-- of Europe and with people that really had no idea of-- of what had happened. And I think what was different for my parents was that they grew up in a community where they had no support network. There were very, very few other survivors. There were a few people that came and left and went to bigger cities for better opportunities. But my parents stuck it out in Duluth, because this is where their relatives were. This is where their family was. As a result, I think they were forced to assimilate very quickly. And I think they were forced to bury a lot of their feelings for a very long period of time. And I think that was probably noteworthy. Outside of that, if you have any other questions, I could answer them for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine.
